---
title: "Siam Weed (Chromolaena odorata)"
slug: "chromolaena-odorata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475018118/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Siam Weed"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475018118/medium.jpg" alt="Siam Weed" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/saherron">
saherron</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264536980">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=199400)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in pinelands, hammocks, and disturbed sites.

## Natural history notes

A fast-growing shrub characterized by its opposite leaves and clusters of fluffy, pale lavender to white flower heads. In Florida, it is found in disturbed areas such as roadsides, fields, and forest edges, particularly in warmer regions. It grows aggressively during the warmer months and is considered an invasive species, capable of forming dense thickets that outcompete native vegetation.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/199400-Chromolaena-odorata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2785)
